name: book-figure-program
description: 'Plan figures that actually teach. Use for figure inventories, chapter-by-chapter visual support, caption consistency, and deciding which concepts need diagrams, scaling plots, or pipeline figures in a technical book.'

user-invocable: true

# Book Figure Program

Use this skill when the manuscript is strong in prose but still needs a coherent visual teaching layer.

## When to Use

- the book has few or no figures yet;
- chapters rely on imagined diagrams;
- you need to decide which figures are mandatory versus optional; and
- caption and figure style need to be standardized.

## Procedure

1. Read the manuscript structure and identify the concepts most likely to benefit from visual explanation.
2. Prefer figures that reduce cognitive load rather than decorate the page.
3. Build a chapter-by-chapter inventory with one pedagogical purpose per figure.
4. Note the likely data source, whether the figure is analytical, schematic, or notebook-generated, and whether the caption must carry extra explanatory load.
5. Keep a coherent visual grammar across the book.

## Figure Types to Prefer

- scaling plots;
- workflow or pipeline diagrams;
- circuit anatomy figures;
- energy landscape sketches;
- comparison figures that make a reality-check point concrete.

## Rules

- Every figure needs a teaching job.
- Captions should explain significance, not just label the image.
- Avoid ornamental figures that do not help the reader recover the argument.

## Recommended Template

- [Figure inventory template](./assets/figure-inventory-template.md)
